content:
y in a story to solve problems, whereas a soft magic system (mysterious, unexplained) should mainly serve to create atmosphere or minor twists, not resolve the core conflict. It’s about earning the reader’s trust: if magic has rules that the audience knows, then using those rules to resolve conflict is fair play. More broadly in design, it’s akin to system transparency: games or stories are more satisfying when the audience/player has learned the rules and then sees them applied in novel ways to overcome challenges. This law highlights a meta-concept of limitations > power: limitations and known rules in a system allow for creative problem-solving (Sanderson’s Second Law explicitly states “Limitations > Powers” in terms of interesting story use of magic). Cross-domain, one could apply this to sci-fi tech as well – e.g., if Star Trek establishes how teleporters or warp drive work, solutions using them feel earned, but pulling out a new tech babble solution at the last second feels cheap. It’s essentially about chekhov’s gun in magic form – foreshadow the capabilities, then use them.
• Balancing Triad (Rock-Paper-Scissors Mechanics) (Domain: Game Design) – A game design principle where three (or more) options are balanced in a cyclical dominance: A beats B, B beats C, C beats A. This ensures no one strategy dominates, encouraging dynamic gameplay. Examples: The classic rock-paper-scissors game itself is a direct model. In many video games or tabletop games: e.g., in strategy war games, cavalry > archers (because they can run them down), archers > spearmen (outrange them), spearmen > cavalry (pikes stop charge). In Pokemon, the elemental types have a rock-paper-scissors style chart (water beats fire, fire beats grass, grass beats water, etc.), requiring players to strategize team composition. Implications: The RPS mechanic is a balance law ensuring that for every strength there is a counter. It prevents stagnation where one dominant strategy wins all. This concept can be generalized to more complex balance, but the essence is asymmetrical balance – differences that keep each other in check in a loop. This appears in economics (paper covers rock? Perhaps analogous to how different market strategies can counter each other), and in evolution (predator
